title: Characteristics of alternating currents
summary: Master AC characteristics for 9702 Physics P4. Learn about peak and RMS values, frequency, and period with expert explanations and worked examples.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between direct current (d.c.) and alternating current (a.c.).
Define and apply peak value, RMS value, frequency, and period to sinusoidal AC waveforms.
Recall and use the relationships I_rms = I_0 / √2 and V_rms = V_0 / √2 for sinusoidal AC.

simpleExplanation.title: AC vs. DC: The Power Play
simpleExplanation.summary: Alternating current (AC) is like a tide, constantly changing direction, unlike direct current (DC) which flows steadily one way. Understanding AC means knowing its maximum 'push' (peak value) and its effective power (RMS value) to safely and efficiently use electricity in your home and beyond.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a child on a swing. DC is like pushing them constantly in one direction (until they hit the ground!). AC is like pushing them back and forth rhythmically. The 'peak value' is the strongest push you give, making the swing reach its maximum height. The 'RMS value' is the equivalent steady push that would keep the swing at the same effective average height, even though the AC push is constantly varying.
steps: Grasp the fundamental difference between alternating and direct current.
Memorise the definitions and graphical representations of peak value, period, and frequency.
Understand the concept and importance of Root-Mean-Square (RMS) values for AC.
Practise applying the RMS formulae (I_rms = I_0/√2, V_rms = V_0/√2) in calculations.

sections.content: Welcome to **Topic 21.1: Characteristics of Alternating Currents**! This is a fundamental topic in A Level Physics, crucial for understanding how electrical power is generated, transmitted, and used in our daily lives. From the **mains supply** in your home to advanced electronics, **alternating current (AC)** plays a vital role. Get ready to master key terms like **peak value**, **RMS value**, **frequency**, and **period**!
What's the Difference: AC vs. DC?

At its core, the distinction between AC and DC lies in the direction of current flow. 

**Direct Current (DC)**:
*   Flows in **one constant direction** through a circuit.
*   Its magnitude can be constant (e.g., from a battery) or vary (e.g., pulsed DC), but the direction remains the same.
*   Examples: Batteries, solar cells, USB chargers.

**Alternating Current (AC)**:
*   Periodically **reverses its direction** of flow.
*   Its magnitude also changes over time, typically in a **sinusoidal** (sine wave) pattern.
*   The most common form of electricity used in homes and businesses, generated at power stations.
*   Examples: Mains electricity supply (e.g., 230 V, 50 Hz in the UK).

When dealing with sinusoidal AC voltage or current, there are several key quantities you must understand and be able to interpret from graphs:

1.  **Peak Value (V₀ or I₀):** This is the **maximum instantaneous voltage or current** reached during one cycle. It's the amplitude of the sinusoidal waveform. It represents the highest 'push' or 'flow' in either direction.

2.  **Period (T):** This is the **time taken for one complete cycle** of the alternating current or voltage. It's measured in seconds (s).

3.  **Frequency (f):** This is the **number of complete cycles** that occur per unit time. It's typically measured in Hertz (Hz), where 1 Hz means one cycle per second.

These three terms are intrinsically linked:
For example, the mains supply in the UK has a frequency of 50 Hz, meaning it completes 50 cycles every second. Its period would therefore be T = 1/50 = 0.02 s.

While the peak value tells you the maximum, it doesn't represent the *effective* or average power delivered by an AC supply, because the instantaneous current and voltage are constantly changing (and even zero twice per cycle!). This is where the **Root-Mean-Square (RMS) value** comes in.

The RMS value of an alternating current or voltage is defined as **the value of steady direct current or voltage that would dissipate energy at the same average rate in a given resistor.** In simpler terms, if a 230 V AC supply has an RMS value of 230 V, it will produce the same average heating effect in a resistor as a 230 V DC supply. This is why appliance ratings (like the 230 V for your wall socket) are always given as RMS values.

For a sinusoidal waveform, the RMS values are related to the peak values by the following equations:
Where √2 is approximately 1.414. This means the RMS value is roughly 70.7% of the peak value.

items: Remember that **appliance ratings** (e.g., 230 V mains) are always given as **RMS values**, not peak values.
Always read the axes on any given AC waveform graph carefully to distinguish between instantaneous values (V or I), peak values (V₀ or I₀), and whether time (t) or phase angle (ωt) is plotted.
The RMS value is crucial because power dissipation depends on I²R or V²/R. A simple average of a sinusoidal AC waveform over a full cycle would be zero, which is unhelpful for energy considerations.
Ensure you can rearrange the RMS formulae to find peak values if given RMS, i.e., I₀ = I_rms × √2 and V₀ = V_rms × √2.
Be careful not to confuse frequency (f) with angular frequency (ω), which is 2πf. While related, they are distinct quantities.

sections.question:
An alternating voltage supply has a peak voltage of 325 V and a frequency of 50 Hz. 

(a) Calculate the RMS voltage of the supply. 
(b) Determine the period of this alternating voltage. 
(c) If this voltage is applied across a 100 Ω resistor, calculate the peak current and the RMS current through the resistor.
sections.solution:
(a) To calculate the RMS voltage, we use the formula:
V_rms = V₀ / √2
V_rms = 325 V / √2
V_rms = 325 V / 1.4142...
V_rms ≈ 229.8 V

(b) To determine the period, we use the relationship between period and frequency:
T = 1/f
T = 1 / 50 Hz
T = 0.02 s

(c) First, calculate the peak current (I₀) using Ohm's Law with the peak voltage:
I₀ = V₀ / R
I₀ = 325 V / 100 Ω
I₀ = 3.25 A

Now, calculate the RMS current (I_rms) using the relationship with peak current:
I_rms = I₀ / √2
I_rms = 3.25 A / √2
I_rms = 3.25 A / 1.4142...
I_rms ≈ 2.30 A

Alternatively, you could first find V_rms (from part a) and then use Ohm's Law:
I_rms = V_rms / R
I_rms = 229.8 V / 100 Ω
I_rms ≈ 2.30 A

sections.content: When solving problems involving AC circuits, pay close attention to whether the question refers to peak or RMS values. Many students lose marks by mixing these up or using peak values when RMS values are implied (e.g., for power calculations). Always explicitly state which value you are calculating.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: What is the key difference between AC and DC current in Cambridge Physics 9702?
faq.a: The key difference is that direct current (DC) flows in one constant direction, while alternating current (AC) periodically reverses its direction of flow. AC also typically varies in magnitude sinusoidally. Understanding this distinction is fundamental for Cambridge 9702 Physics and is covered in our free revision course and past paper practice.

faq.q: Why do we use RMS values for AC, and how does it relate to power for 9702?
faq.a: We use Root-Mean-Square (RMS) values for AC because they represent the *effective* value of the alternating current or voltage. The RMS value is the equivalent DC value that would dissipate energy at the same average rate in a given resistor. This makes RMS values crucial for calculating average power and understanding how AC supplies compare to DC. Our free 9702 Physics course delves into this with clear explanations and past paper examples.

faq.q: How do I calculate the frequency of an AC supply from a graph for Cambridge 9702 Physics?
faq.a: To calculate the frequency from an AC waveform graph, first identify the period (T), which is the time taken for one complete cycle. Once you have the period in seconds, the frequency (f) is simply the reciprocal of the period: f = 1/T. Ensure you read the time axis scale carefully. Our premium lessons include worked examples on interpreting AC graphs for Cambridge 9702.
